# FRAMEWORK PDF CAPTIONS — bgd-cyclone 2025-04-25
# framework_doc: https://www.unocha.org/publications/report/bangladesh/anticipatory-action-framework-bangladesh-cyclones-2025-version
# pages: 24 · model: sonnet · via: claude headless
# Auto-generated descriptions of the PDF's visuals (maps/charts/tables). PUBLIC.
------------------------------------------------------------
--- page 1 ---
Main point: Cover page and Table of Contents for the Anticipatory Action Framework – Bangladesh Cyclones, 2025 Version.
- Title: "Anticipatory Action Framework – Bangladesh Cyclones, 2025 Version"
- Approved by Resident Coordinator: 7 April 2025; Endorsed by USG/ERC: 25 April 2025
- Table of Contents sections: Executive summary, Current structure / activation protocol, Introduction (Risk/Hazard, Anticipatory Action, Prior AA in Bangladesh, Overarching framework), The Model: Forecast and Triggers (Forecast, Trigger, Edge cases, Early Response, Monitoring steps, Roles and responsibilities, Challenges), The Delivery: Action Plan (FAO, UNFPA, UNICEF, WFP interventions), The Money – Pre-arranged Funds (Financing overview, CERF, Challenges), The Learning (M&E, Independent Evaluation), Final provisions, Annexes 1–4 (Historical Analysis, Distribution list, Trigger Messages, Overview of Activities)

--- page 2 ---
Main point: Executive summary and activation protocol overview; total pre-arranged funding ~$8M.
- Framework initiated January 2023, revised March 2025; targets Bay of Bengal cyclones
- Total pre-arranged funds: $8,026,987 ($4,000,000 CERF + $4,026,987 from BDRCS, BRAC, STEP, UNFPA, UNICEF, WFP)
- Cyclone seasons monitored: April–June and October–November
- Activation protocol summary:
  • Readiness trigger: monitoring team sends communication 3–5 days before landfall; CERF disburses full amount; partners implement readiness activities only
  • Action trigger: further communication sent; full AA implementation begins immediately, no further approval required
  • Missing readiness: if action trigger reached without prior readiness trigger, disbursement and implementation start simultaneously
  • Observational trigger: activated only if action trigger was NOT reached; covers cases where cyclone causes severe impact despite sub-threshold forecast; agencies authorised to spend 100% of funds; window up to 4 weeks after landfall

--- page 3 ---
Main point: AA activation workflow diagram showing three sequential phases with decision branches.
- Figure: Flow diagram with vertical timeline (top = earliest, bottom = latest)
- Phase 1 – Readiness (3–5 days before landfall):
  • Continuous cyclone forecast monitoring → if readiness trigger threshold reached → readiness activation notification to partners → full funding disbursement from CERF; partners implement readiness activities
- Phase 2 – Action (0–3 days; government cut-off 12 hours before landfall):
  • Continuous monitoring → if action trigger threshold reached → activation notification → full implementation of AA activities
- Phase 3 – Early Response (up to 4 weeks after landfall):
  • Applies only if action trigger NOT reached
  • Monitoring of observational data → if observational trigger threshold reached → notification → disbursement of funds and/or early response activities
- Phases separated by dashed horizontal lines; readiness and action phases can overlap

--- page 4 ---
Main point: Introduction – Bangladesh cyclone risk context and anticipatory action definition; narrative text, no figure.
- Bangladesh cyclone season: April–June and October–November
- Severe cyclone every 3 years on average (Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change)
- BMD data: 10 cyclones impacted coastal districts 1991–2023; 6 tropical cyclones with wind speeds >118 km/h in Bay of Bengal
- AA framework comprises 3 core elements: the Model (forecast + trigger), the Delivery (action plan), the Money (pre-arranged finance)
- BDRCS pioneered cyclone AA approach in 2015; trigger thresholds and Early Action Protocol (EAP) developed for monsoon floods

--- page 5 ---
Main point: History of prior AA activations in Bangladesh, including 2020 and 2024 flood framework activations; narrative text, no figure.
- 2015, 2017, 2019: BDRCS/WFP piloted AA cash assistance for monsoon floods
- 4 July 2020: CERF-funded OCHA flood AA triggered; 220,000+ people received assistance before life-threatening flooding; WFP, FAO, UNFPA worked with BDRCS and 10 NGOs
- 2021: Revised AA framework endorsed by RC; covered 5 highly vulnerable districts: Bogura, Gaibandha, Kurigram, Jamalpur, Sirajganj (Jamuna River basin)
- 2023: Framework expanded to include Jamuna + Padma River basin
- July 2024: Collective floods framework triggered; $6.2M from CERF released immediately; $2.3M additional from partners; >80% of assistance delivered within 5 days of activation; 500,000+ people reached before peak flooding
- 2024 activation deliverables: multi-purpose cash transfers, water purification, agricultural support, dignity/baby kits, GBV information services

--- page 6 ---
Main point: Overarching framework principles and trigger model specification (wind threshold = 118 km/h, 1-in-4.75-year return period); narrative text, no figure.
- RC leads overall coordination; framework anchored in existing in-country mechanisms (AA-TWG, NAWG, ICCG, HAG, HCTT)
- Forecast sources: BMD, IMD (India Meteorological Department), ECMWF, GFS, Global Forecast System, JTWC
- Monitoring: twice daily during cyclone season
- Trigger threshold: 1-in-4.75-year return period or greater = wind speed ≥118 km/h in target coastal districts
  • Corresponds to: ≥25% household asset damage OR ≥40% of population in a union affected
- Stage I – Readiness (3–5 days lead time):
  • At least 2 models from {BMD, IMD, ECMWF, GFS, JTWC} forecast ≥118 km/h in coastal pilot districts
- Stage II – Action (≤72 hours lead time):
  • Well-formed low pressure or depression in Bay of Bengal AND ≥2 models from same set forecast ≥118 km/h in coastal pilot districts within 72 hours
- Stage III – Observational Trigger (at/after landfall):
  • Observed wind speed ≥118 km/h from BMD or IMD OR ≥2 models from {ECMWF, GFS, JTWC} confirm ≥118 km/h at landfall/crossover
  • Only activates if action trigger was NOT previously reached

--- page 7 ---
Main point: Trigger criteria detail, edge-case handling, early response procedures, and monitoring team steps; narrative text, no figure.
- 50% chance readiness and action triggers do not coincide (i.e., one reached without the other)
- Edge case: if activation occurs with <24 hours lead time, agencies assess safety before proceeding; RC decides in consultation with MoDMR; agencies must not implement if safety of staff or beneficiaries is at risk; government closure of facilities within 12 hours of landfall must be respected
- Early Response: if observational trigger reached, geographical reprioritisation may be needed; AA activities are largely dual-use (also eligible for immediate post-shock response); additional changes processed via standard CERF project revision
- Monitoring steps (performed by monitoring team – Ahmadul Hassan, Pauline Ndirangu [OCHA Data Scientist], Leonardo Milano [OCHA Data Science Team Lead]):
  1. Calculate distance from expected landfall location to target unions
  2. Apply wind speed reduction factor (from IFRC DREF EAP) to estimate wind speed per union
  3. Identify unions/upazilas exceeding activation threshold
  4. Verify trigger criteria against trigger statement
  5. Produce situation report for OCHA AA partners

--- page 8 ---
Main point: Roles and responsibilities table for the AA activation process; table with 4 columns (Who, What, How, When).
- Row 1: AA Working Group – Cyclone focal point / Monitoring team | BMD/IMD/ECMWF/GFS/JTWC forecast analysis | Populates forecast log twice daily | As soon as forecast produced, before truncated to 72 hours
- Row 2: AA Working Group – Cyclone focal point / Monitoring team | Evaluate trigger; send email to distribution list | Communicates 5-day readiness, 72-hour action, or observational trigger status to HCTT members, RC/RCO, UN agencies, OCHA, partners via email + WhatsApp; includes intervention map | —
- Row 3: CERF Secretariat | When readiness or action/observational trigger reached (or action trigger without prior readiness), immediately sends pre-approval letters to UN agencies | Based on endorsed framework + CERF application | As triggers are reached
- Row 4: RC | Can pause activation upon consultation with MoDMR (outlier case where storm track would cause minimal impact) | Direct contact between RC and MoDMR | Once framework is activated, in outlier cases
- Row 5: MoDMR | Can discuss pause with RC | Direct contact (facilitated by OCHA HoO if needed) | Once framework activated, in outlier cases
- Challenges noted: need for impact-based forecasting; short lead times; wind-speed-only trigger (storm surge and rainfall not included); potential future integration with monsoon flood framework

--- page 9 ---
Main point: District vulnerability ranking table (8 coastal districts) and Action Plan overview; key table.
- Table: Districts ranked by composite score (INFORM Natural Hazard + INFORM Vulnerability + frequency of cyclones ≥118 km/h, 1977–2023)
  Rank | District    | INFORM Nat. Hazard | INFORM Vulnerability | Freq (Cat.1+) | Score
  1    | Satkhira    | 6.9                | 5.5                  | 0.1304        | 4.95
  2    | Patuakhali  | 5.6                | 6.7                  | 0.1304        | 4.89
  3    | Bhola       | 5.0                | 5.8                  | 0.1522        | 4.41
  4    | Noakhali    | 5.9                | 5.6                  | 0.1304        | 4.31
  5    | Barguna     | 7.2                | 6.4                  | 0.0870        | 4.01
  6    | Khulna      | 6.0                | 5.9                  | 0.0870        | 3.08
  7    | Pirojpur    | 5.4                | 6.1                  | 0.0870        | 2.86
  8    | Bagerhat    | 6.0                | 6.5                  | 0.0652        | 2.54
- CERF funding currently limited to top 5–6 most exposed districts: Satkhira, Patuakhali, Bhola, Noakhali, Barguna, and Khulna
- All 8 sea-facing districts may receive AA activities from non-CERF partners

--- page 10 ---
Main point: FAO intervention details — 5,550 farming households, 4 districts, cash grants USD 41.32 / BDT 5,000; narrative text, no figure.
- FAO objective: protect livelihood/agriculture assets in cyclone-prone coastal areas
- Target: 5,550 farming households in Satkhira, Barguna, Patuakhali, Noakhali; early warning to 25,000 households (106,500 people)
- Readiness phase activities:
  • EWM via voice + SMS to 106,500 people (25,000 HH) + loudspeaker at community/union/shelter level
  • Government partner sensitisation
  • Cyclone shelter preparation for cattle management (with Shelter Management Committee, LDMC, DLS)
  • Community mobilisation for livestock evacuation
  • Procurement of animal feed
- Action phase activities (if forecast-based trigger reached pre-landfall):
  • EWM repeated to 106,500 people
  • Animal feed provision for evacuated cattle at 60 shelters (~200 cattle per shelter)
  • Shelter-based cattle management with DLS
  • Unconditional cash grants: USD 41.32 / BDT 5,000 via Mobile Money Transfer to 5,550 farming HH (for transport/evacuation/animal shelter/repair/destocking)
  • IEC materials on livestock management in emergencies
  • Post-distribution monitoring + participation in independent evaluation

--- page 11 ---
Main point: UNFPA and UNICEF intervention descriptions — GBV/SRH in 2 districts, WASH/Health/cash in 5 districts; narrative text, no figure.
- UNFPA: targets 9,050 at-risk women and adolescent girls (including 2,000 pregnant + 200 trans women) in Patuakhali and Noakhali
  • 2,500 dignity kits (women + transgender)
  • 9 MHPSS counsellors deployed to brothels + shelters
  • Community Women Support Volunteers (CWSVs) deployed in Noakhali + Patuakhali (safe evacuation, PSEA)
  • 2,000 MHM (Menstrual Health Management) kits + two-way transport cash to adolescent girls (10–19 years); covers ≥3 months of menstrual cycles
  • 1,500 MHH (Menstrual Health and Hygiene) cash transfers to adolescent girls (10–19)
  • 2,000 baby kits to pregnant women
  • Midwives deployed to shelters/union facilities
  • Cash for obstetric care: 50 pregnant women with complications
  • Lifesaving SRHR/GBV information at evacuation centres
- UNICEF: targets up to 150,000 people in 5 districts: Bhola, Barguna, Patuakhali (Barisal Division); Noakhali (Chattogram Division); Satkhira (Khulna Division)
  • Sectors: WASH, Health, Multi-Purpose Cash
  • Aim: reduce risk of life-threatening WASH diseases (acute watery diarrhoea); education continuity; cash for resilience

--- page 12 ---
Main point: UNICEF activity specifics + WFP intervention (35,920 HH cash via bKash, BDT 5,000) + Money section intro; narrative text, no figure.
- UNICEF multi-sectoral activities:
  • Life-saving WASH supplies (WPTs, jerrycans)
  • 20 mobile water treatment plants at evacuation/cyclone shelter points
  • Hygiene kits including menstrual hygiene management materials
  • Mobile medical teams + volunteers + essential medicines
  • Vaccines, medicines, cold chain, medical equipment moved to higher floors (storm protection)
  • Relocation of pregnant women, newborns, critically ill
  • Cash assistance: BDT 5,000 / USD 41.32 per HH via mobile money transfer
- WFP: focuses on Khulna, Satkhira, Patuakhali, Barguna, Bhola
  • Pre-readiness: 45,000 HH selected; bKash account verification
  • Upon activation: 35,920 HH receive one-off unconditional cash transfer of BDT 5,000 (US$41.25) via bKash before cyclone; value = 75% of Minimum Expenditure Basket (MEB) for 5-member household
  • CERF-funded: fortified biscuits to 25,000 HH (125,000 people) at cyclone shelters
  • EWM in local languages including remote villages, jointly with CPP volunteers
- Pre-arranged funds total: $9,951,987 (CERF $4M + $5,951,987 co-funding from BDRCS, BRAC, STEP, UNFPA, UNICEF, Islamic Relief, Save the Children, WFP)

--- page 13 ---
Main point: Financing overview table — total $9,951,987 across 10 agencies; key table.
- Table: Agency | CERF Funding (US$) | Co-funding (US$) | Total (US$) | Remarks
  FAO          | 500,000    | —          | 500,000    | —
  UNFPA        | 300,000    | 15,930     | 315,930    | —
  UNICEF       | 1,100,000  | 1,900,000  | 3,000,000  | —
  WFP          | 2,100,000  | 1,600,000  | 3,700,000  | —
  BDRCS        | n/a        | 441,950    | 464,047    | DREF
                             | 22,097     |            | Internal (quick fund, $4,000/district)
  BRAC         | n/a        | 8,500      | 8,500      | Internal fund
  STEP         | n/a        | 38,510     | 38,510     | Tentative
  Start Network| n/a        | 1,775,000  | 1,775,000  | Start Ready
  Islamic Relief| n/a       | 64,000     | 64,000     | (50,000 GBP)
  Save the Children | n/a   | 150,000    | 150,000    | ECHO + GFFO + SC Humanitarian Fund
  TOTAL        | 4,000,000  | 5,951,987  | 9,951,987  | —
- CERF US$4M disbursed on no-regrets basis upon any trigger activation (readiness, action, or observational), within 2 years of ERC pre-approval
- Readiness vs action cost categories must be pre-defined in project proposals

--- page 14 ---
Main point: CERF disbursement rules, financing challenges, and The Learning section objectives; narrative text, no figure.
- CERF funds partially usable at readiness; remainder only upon action or observational trigger
- Pre-arranged AA funding does not preclude additional CERF rapid-response funding
- Financing challenges:
  • "Build costs" (pre-activation framework setup) not traditionally covered by CERF or pooled funds
  • Limited preparedness funds for goods prepositioning constrains scale
  • AA cannot cover all impacts; linkages with emergency response and early recovery need strengthening
- The Learning section — MEAL committee convened by OCHA with partners:
  • Key learning questions: Timing (did early action make a difference?), Joint targeting (did common beneficiary database improve outcomes?), Early warning (effectiveness + content of messages?), Localisation (did AA enable greater localisation?), Partnership (lessons with government?), Pre-crisis survey (risks that AA can mitigate), Actions (agency-level improvements), Effectiveness (impact reduction?), AAP (community participation level)

--- page 15 ---
Main point: M&E, Independent Evaluation, and Advocacy provisions; narrative text, no figure.
- Each agency uses existing M&E systems; data shared with OCHA
- Common M&E questionnaires coordinated across agencies to reduce beneficiary interview burden
- MEAL experts at global level consulted; guidance drawn from published anticipatory action evaluation literature
- Independent evaluation: all agencies agree to participate; may include sharing of beneficiary data; ad hoc committee oversees scope
- Advocacy: coordinated under RC leadership; on activation: joint press release + mutual social media amplification
- Agencies share photos, videos, and impact stories with ad hoc committee

--- page 16 ---
Main point: Annex 1 — Historical cyclone data table (1991–2023), used in return period calculations against 118 km/h threshold.
- Table 1: Historical cyclone impact data for Bangladesh coastal areas
  Year | Name   | Wind speed (km/h) | Storm surge height (m) | Affected population | Affected houses (fully damaged) | Loss of life | Livestock
  1991 | Bob 1  | 250               | 6.5                    | 15,000,000          | 854,399                         | 138,882      | 700,000
  2007 | Sidr   | 223               | 4.57                   | 6,737,815           | 564,967                         | >10,000      | 382,000
  2009 | Aila   | 120               | 3.05                   | 3,900,000           | 243,000                         | 190          | NA
  2015 | Komen  | 65                | 1–2                    | 2,600,000           | 88,900                          | 9            | N/A
  2016 | Roanu  | 90                | 1.5                    | 1,297,010           | 216,771                         | 24           | NA
  2017 | Mora   | 146               | 1.5                    | 3,300,000           | 17,023                          | 7            | NA
  2019 | Fani   | 88                | 1.5                    | 1,600,000           | 2,363                           | 17           | 175
  2019 | Bulbul | 140               | 1.83                   | 2,100,000           | 16,580                          | 25           | NA
  2020 | Amphan | 151               | 3–5                    | 2,600,000           | 55,667                          | 26           | NA
  2021 | Yaas   | 150               | 1.8–2.4                | 1,300,000           | 26,000                          | 9            | NA
  2022 | Sitrang| 88                | 1–2.4                  | 1,000,000           | 20,000                          | 35           | NA
  2023 | Mocha  | 145               | 1.5–2                  | 750,000             | 17,000                          | 0            | NA

--- page 17 ---
Main point: Map 1 — Bangladesh district-level cyclone hit frequency 1977–2023 (wind >118 km/h, Cat I+), used for pilot district prioritisation.
- Map: All Bangladesh districts labelled; southern/coastal districts colour-coded by cyclone hit frequency
- Variable: Frequency of cyclone events with wind speed >118 km/h (Category I and above), 1977–2023
- Legend scale: 2 (light yellow) → 3 (orange) → 4 (amber) → 5 (orange-red) → 6 (red) → 7 (dark red)
- Highest frequency districts (darkest, ~6–7 events): Barguna and adjacent coastal south
- High frequency (5–6 events): Patuakhali, Bhola, Noakhali, Satkhira, Bagerhat, Pirojpur
- Moderate (3–4 events): Khulna, Jessore, Feni, Chittagong, Cox's Bazar
- Low (2 events): scattered inland districts
- Box labelled "consider in analysis" highlights Bandarbon and Cox's Bazar area at bottom right
- Interior/northern districts (Dhaka, Rajshahi, Sylhet divisions): no colouring, zero frequency

--- page 18 ---
Main point: Wind speed reduction factor models (exponential decay by distance) and household asset damage curves by wall type and coastal zone.
- Graph 1 left (Cyclone Amphan 2020): Wind Velocity Reduction Factor vs Distance from Landfall (km)
  • X-axis: 0–600 km; Y-axis: Reduction factor 0–1
  • Fitted equation: y = 0.9807e^(−0.002x), R² = 0.7695
  • Source: BMD & IMD
- Graph 1 right (Cyclone Mocha 2023): Wind Velocity Reduction Factor vs Distance (km)
  • X-axis: 0–160 km; Y-axis: 0–1.00
  • Fitted equation: y = 1.0656e^(−0.037x), R² = 0.9678
- Graph 2 left – Central Coastal Zone: HH asset damage (%) vs wind speed (km/h), 0–250 km/h
  • Two curves: Kacha (tin roof and wall) — steep rise, ~100% at ~200 km/h; Semi-Pucca (tin roof + brick wall) — slower rise
- Graph 2 right – Western Coastal Zone: same axes and curve types; similar damage trajectory
- Table 2: Damage percentage at 118 km/h wind speed
  Zone    | Semi-Pucca (70%) | Kacha (30%) | Weighted average impact
  Central | 30%              | 40%         | 30%
  Western | 20%              | 45%         | 25%
  Note: 200 HH surveyed by BDRCS/GRC; wind >118 km/h causes structural damage to >25% of houses
- Table 3 (partial, header only): Cyclone forecast models — Forecast | Content | Lead time | Skill

--- page 19 ---
Main point: Forecast model specifications and performance error table across 24/48/72-hour lead times; key table.
- Table 3 continued — Forecast model details (Source: EAP Cyclone, IFRC):
  Model | Content                              | Lead time              | Skill
  BMD   | Windspeed + Storm Surge (not loc-specific) | DD:48hr, CS:30hr  | Good at 1,000 km from coast; storm surge needs improvement
  IMD   | Track + wind speed + tentative storm surge | DD:48–120hr, SCS:48hr+ | Good track after CS formation; storm surge improvement needed
  ECMWF | Wind and storm surge                  | 10 days               | Good after DD formation
  GFS   | Wind and storm surge                  | 10 days               | Good after D/DD formation
  JTWC  | Track + wind speed + tentative storm surge | After DD formation | Good after CS formation
- Performance of forecast models — Average errors:
  Model       | Parameter                              | 24hr    | 48hr    | 72hr
  BMD         | Track Forecast Error (km)              | 60–80   | 80–100  | 100–120
              | Intensity (wind) Error (km/h)          | 15–20   | 20–30   | 20–40
              | Landfall Time Error (hr)               | 1–2     | 3–4     | 4–6
  RSMC-IMD    | Track Forecast Error (km)              | 65      | 90      | 115
              | Intensity Error (km/h)                 | 17      | 20      | 20
              | Landfall Time Error (hr)               | 1.8     | 2.7     | 4.7
  JTWC        | Track Forecast Error (km)              | 24      | 54      | 80
              | Intensity Error (km/h)                 | 13.7    | 12.9    | 14.1
              | Landfall Time Error (hr)               | 3       | 5       | 6
  ECMWF       | Track Forecast Error (km)              | 78      | 103     | 154
              | Intensity Error (km/h)                 | 25      | 40      | 45
              | Landfall Time Error (hr)               | 2.3     | 2.9     | 4.2
  NCEP-GFS    | Track Forecast Error (km)              | 78      | 115     | 170
              | Intensity Error (km/h)                 | 25      | 35      | 45
              | Landfall Time Error (hr)               | 2.2     | 3.9     | 6.7
- Conclusion: Track error beyond 48 hours is very high → models useful for readiness but not for action trigger activation

--- page 20 ---
Main point: Annex 2 — Distribution list for trigger notifications; names and email contacts, no figure.
- Monitoring team: Ahmadul Hassan (research assistant), Pauline Ndirangu (OCHA Data Scientist), Leonardo Milano (OCHA Data Science Team Lead)

--- page 21 ---
Main point: Annex 3 — Template trigger notification messages for readiness, action, and observational triggers; narrative text, no figure.
- Readiness trigger email template:
  • Subject: "Cyclone Anticipatory Action Bangladesh – Readiness Trigger Reached"
  • Key content: trigger reached per BMD/IMD/ECMWF/GFS/JTWC forecasts; wind speeds expected >118 km/h in coastal district unions; CERF funding released; readiness activities commence; OCHA may convene partner meeting
- Action trigger email template:
  • Subject: "Cyclone Anticipatory Action Bangladesh – Activation Trigger Reached"
  • Key content: trigger reached per same model set; all activities commence immediately; encouragement to document lessons; visuals/stories to be shared with group
- Note: Both reference the endorsed framework document via hyperlink

--- page 22 ---
Main point: Observational trigger template message and forecast information template with map + union table placeholders; narrative text, no figure.
- Observational trigger email template:
  • Subject: "Cyclone Anticipatory Action Bangladesh – Observational Trigger Reached"
  • Key content: observed wind speeds at landfall exceeded 118 km/h in coastal district unions per BMD/IMD/ECMWF/GFS/JTWC data; all activities commence
- Forecast information template (sent alongside readiness/action trigger):
  • Subject: "Cyclone Anticipatory Action Bangladesh – Forecast Information"
  • Content: named cyclone forecast produced at [HH:MM, YYYY-MM-DD BDT]; wind speeds expected >118 km/h in coastal district unions
  • Placeholder: [Image of the track from BMD]
  • Placeholder: [Table with unions from monitoring spreadsheet] — lists unions with wind speeds >118 km/h in Barguna, Bhola, Patuakhali, Noakhali, Satkhira

--- page 23 ---
Main point: Annex 4 — Overview of Activities table (part 1): FAO, WFP, and UNICEF WASH activities with funding and people targeted.
- Table columns: Agency | Sector/Cluster | CERF Funds (US$) | Co-fund (US$) | People Targeted | Key Activities
- FAO | Food Security – Agriculture | $500,000 | — | 106,500 |
  • EWM to 25,000 HH (remote farming communities)
  • Shelter-based cattle management + feed distribution (12,000 people at 60 shelter centers)
  • IEC materials on livestock management in emergencies
  • Unconditional cash to protect farming livelihoods (23,643 people)
- WFP | Multi-purpose cash | $2,100,000 | $1,600,000 | 329,000 |
  • Multipurpose cash support; early warning
- UNICEF | WASH | $780,000 | $1,900,000 | 150,000 |
  • Deploy 20 mobile water treatment plants at evacuation points and cyclone shelters
  • Distribution of life-saving WASH supplies (WPTs, jerrycans, soap, sanitary napkins)
  • Minor repair/maintenance/installation of temporary WASH facilities (water supply, sanitation, handwashing) at evacuation/shelter points
  • Mass communication on hygiene (handwashing, latrines, WPT, menstrual hygiene) via media, banners, posters in shelters

--- page 24 ---
Main point: Annex 4 continued — UNICEF Health, Multi-purpose cash, Protection/GBV, and UNFPA Health/SRH activities with funding and people targeted.
- UNICEF | Health | $220,000 | — | 61,191 |
  • Mobile medical teams for quality essential services; lifesaving supplies + medicines
  • Transport services for critically ill patients; referral of pregnant mothers to higher facilities
  • Prepositioning of essential medications; ANC and safe delivery services
- UNICEF | Multi-purpose cash | $99,000 | — | 1,940 |
  • Unconditional multipurpose cash to vulnerable pregnant and lactating mothers with young children (ages 0–4)
- UNICEF | Protection – GBV | $88,235 | $15,930 | 2,500 |
  • 2,500 dignity kits + lifesaving GBV risk mitigation info + referral pathways to women and vulnerable groups
  • 40 trained Women and Girls Support Volunteers for DK distribution + safeguarding + GBV referral; trained to support women with disabilities
  • 10 MHPSS counsellors at evacuation centres; PFA and lifesaving information
- UNFPA | Health / SRH | $211,765 | — | 6,000 |
  • Deploy 30 midwives + 8 medical doctors + 60 volunteers at temporary safe evacuation centres for pregnant women
  • 2,000 baby kits to pregnant women (with transport cash support)
  • 3,000 MHM kits (including transport cash) to adolescent girls including girls with disabilities
  • Cash for Menstrual Health and Hygiene to 1,000 adolescent girls including girls with disabilities
  • 170 youth volunteers at evacuation centres + distribution points; safeguarding message dissemination including EWM and GBV referral pathway; specifically trained to support girls with disabilities and caregivers
